mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Malaysia on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia. Specifically South-East Asia, just north of the equator 🌏

mapQuestions.question: Malaysia is split into two parts. What are they?
mapQuestions.answer: Peninsular Malaysia (joined to mainland Asia) and East Malaysia (on the island of Borneo). The two parts are about 600km apart, separated by the South China Sea.

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Malaysia?
mapQuestions.answer: Kuala Lumpur, often shortened to "KL". It means "muddy confluence", because it grew up where two muddy rivers meet.

mapQuestions.question: Which country sits inside Malaysia?
mapQuestions.answer: Singapore is a tiny island country joined to Peninsular Malaysia by a short bridge. They were the same country until 1965, when Singapore was separated.

mapQuestions.question: Malaysia shares an island with two other countries. Which?
mapQuestions.answer: The island of Borneo is shared by Malaysia, Indonesia, and the tiny oil-rich kingdom of Brunei.

foods.name: Nasi lemak
foods.description: Coconut rice with crispy fried anchovies, peanuts, boiled egg, cucumber, and spicy sambal. The national dish, eaten any time of day, usually for breakfast.

foods.name: Roti canai
foods.description: A flaky, buttery flatbread tossed and stretched until paper-thin, then folded and pan-fried, served with curry or dhal for dipping.

foods.name: Char kway teow
foods.description: Wide flat rice noodles stir-fried hard and fast over flame with prawns, Chinese sausage, egg, bean sprouts and chives. Best from a smoky hawker stall.

foods.name: Laksa
foods.description: A spicy noodle soup. There are dozens of regional kinds. Penang assam laksa is sour, fishy and herby. Curry laksa is creamy with coconut. Both are amazing.

foods.name: Satay
foods.description: Charcoal-grilled skewers of chicken or beef marinated in turmeric, served with thick peanut sauce, cucumber and rice cakes. Eaten by the dozen.

foods.name: Cendol
foods.description: A cold dessert of shaved ice, coconut milk, palm sugar syrup and bright green jelly noodles made from rice flour. The perfect antidote to the heat.

scavengerItems.label: The Petronas Twin Towers (or a souvenir of them)
A hawker stall with stools so small you nearly miss them
A mosque, a temple and a church within walking distance of each other
A palm-oil plantation stretching to the horizon (from a car window)
A monkey on a roof, fence or wire
A sudden afternoon thunderstorm out of a clear sky
A type of banana you have never seen before
A trishaw (especially in Melaka, often decorated with flowers and speakers)
A "wau bulan" moon kite (a Malaysian symbol you'll see on signs and money)
A sign in three different scripts (Latin, Chinese, Tamil) on the same wall

geoMatches.description: The capital. Skyscrapers next to colonial buildings next to mosques next to Chinese temples. The Petronas Twin Towers light up at night.
An island in the north-west, with the city of George Town. UNESCO heritage shophouses, colourful street art, and arguably the best food in Asia.
East Malaysia, shared with Indonesia and Brunei. Rainforests with wild orangutans, the world's biggest flowers, and the highest mountain in South-East Asia.
An archipelago of 99 islands in the Andaman Sea. Jungle-covered rocks rising out of the sea, eagles overhead, sandy beaches.
A small old port city on the west coast. Once ruled by Malays, Portuguese, Dutch and British. Red colonial buildings, river boats, and Peranakan houses.
A cool tea-growing region in the misty hills. Endless rows of bright green tea bushes, strawberry farms, and cool nights, a relief from the lowland heat.

senses.smell: Frying garlic and shrimp paste at a hawker stall, frangipani, durian (you will never forget it), incense from Chinese temples, monsoon rain on hot streets…
senses.hear: The clang of woks, scooters revving, the call to prayer five times a day, Tamil pop blaring from a kopitiam radio, monsoon rain hammering on tin roofs…
senses.taste: Coconut rice with crunchy peanuts, fiery sambal, sweet teh tarik (pulled milk tea), salty fried anchovies, cooling cendol, the punch of fresh chilli…
senses.feel: Sticky humidity, the cool of an air-conditioned mall after the street, hot plastic stools at hawker centres, the soft splash of warm-sea waves…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why does Malaysian food taste like three different countries at once?
stories.body: Because it kind of is. Malaysia is home to three big communities living side by side: Malays (mostly Muslim, descended from people who have lived here for thousands of years), Chinese (whose ancestors came in the 1800s to work tin mines), and Indians (whose ancestors came mainly from Tamil Nadu to work rubber plantations). Each brought their own food, language, religion and festivals. Over time the cuisines borrowed from each other. So Malaysian food might use Chinese noodles, Indian curry spices and Malay coconut milk in the same dish, and it works.

stories.question: Why is Penang so obsessed with food?
stories.body: In George Town, the capital of Penang, food is a near-religion. Hawker stalls have been in the same families for three or four generations, with grandparents passing down a single noodle recipe perfected over 50 years. Most stalls only make one dish. Locals will queue 40 minutes for char kway teow at a particular stall but walk past three others. Streets are named after the food sold on them. The unofficial way to greet someone is "Eat already?" (Sudah makan?), and the answer is expected to be no, so you can join them.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If your family was famous for cooking just one dish for the next 50 years, what should it be?

stories.question: What is the biggest flower in the world?
stories.body: It grows in the rainforests of Borneo and Sumatra. The rafflesia can reach over a metre wide, weigh 10 kilos, and has no leaves, no stem and no roots, it is a parasite that grows out of a jungle vine. It blooms for just five days, then rots into a black puddle. And to top it off, it smells like rotting flesh, on purpose, to attract flies that pollinate it. People in Borneo trek through jungle for hours just to see one in bloom, then have to leave very quickly because of the smell.

stories.question: Who are the wild people of Borneo?
stories.body: "Orangutan" comes from the Malay words "orang" (person) and "hutan" (forest), so it literally means "person of the forest". They live wild only in Borneo and Sumatra, and they share about 97% of their DNA with humans. They are smart enough to use tools, build nests in trees every night, and they can recognise faces. Sadly, their forest is being cut down for palm oil plantations, and there may be only around 100,000 of them left in the wild. The Sepilok rehabilitation centre in Borneo rescues babies who have lost their mothers and teaches them how to climb and find food.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Orangutans share 97% of our DNA. What is one human thing they do that surprises you most?

stories.question: Why is one fruit banned in hotels?
stories.body: Durian is a big, spiky green fruit that Malaysians call "the king of fruits". The flesh inside is sweet, creamy and rich, and some people would happily fight a tiger for one. The catch: it smells. Really smells. People have compared it to gym socks, blue cheese, rotting onions, or all three at once. The smell is so strong, and it clings to fabric so well, that you will see "No durian" signs in hotels, airports and trains all over South-East Asia. Whole shops sell nothing but durian. Try a piece if you dare, just maybe wash your hands afterwards.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Imagine inventing a fruit that smells terrible but tastes amazing. Who would you trick into trying it first?

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in Malaysia?
Did you try durian? On a scale of 1 to 10, how bad / good was it?
Malaysians speak Malay, English, Chinese and Tamil. How does it feel to be somewhere where everyone speaks several languages?
Which has been your favourite place so far, the city, the jungle, or the beach?
What's one thing Malaysians do better than back home?
Three big communities live side by side here, mostly peacefully. What do you think makes that work?

wordSearch.hint: Malaysian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Long-tailed macaque
animals.description: In every city park and every roadside stop. Will absolutely steal your lunch.

animals.name: House gecko
animals.description: On every wall, in every shop. Tick-tick-tick at night.

animals.name: Rajah Brooke's birdwing
animals.description: Huge black-and-iridescent-green butterfly of Borneo rainforests.

animals.name: Green sea turtle
animals.description: Nests on Sabah's Turtle Islands. Lights-out beaches in nesting season.

animals.name: Bornean orangutan
animals.description: In Sepilok sanctuary (Sabah) or the Kinabatangan river. Critically endangered.

animals.name: Bearded pig
animals.description: A wild pig native to Borneo, with bristly white whiskers. In Bako National Park.
